summary:
The Sea Peoples were a confederation of maritime raiders and migrants who swept through the eastern Mediterranean between approximately 1200 and 1150 BC, leaving destruction in their wake and contributing to the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]] — one of the most catastrophic civilizational disruptions in ancient history. Their origins remain one of the great unsolved problems of ancient history, though they almost certainly came from diverse regions including the Aegean, Anatolia, Cyprus, and possibly the western Mediterranean.

Egyptian records, particularly inscriptions and reliefs from the reigns of Merneptah (c. 1213–1203 BC) and Ramesses III (c. 1184–1153 BC) at Medinet Habu, provide the most detailed contemporary accounts. The texts describe multiple groups — the Peleset, Tjeker, Shekelesh, Denyen, and Weshesh among others — who swept across the eastern Mediterranean by land and sea, destroying the cities of Hatti (the [[event:54|Hittite Empire]]), Cyprus, [[event:1833|Ugarit]], and the Levantine coast before being repulsed by Egyptian forces in two major battles, one on land and one at sea.

The impact of the Sea Peoples was devastating and transformative. The [[event:54|Hittite Empire]] was destroyed and never recovered. The thriving Late Bronze Age palace civilization of Cyprus collapsed. The great trading city of [[event:1833|Ugarit]] was burned and permanently abandoned around 1185 BC. Dozens of cities across the Levant and Aegean were destroyed or abandoned. The [[event:57|Mycenaean civilization]] collapsed at roughly the same time, plunging Greece into the [[event:1826|Greek Dark Ages]].

The Sea Peoples who settled in Canaan after being repelled from Egypt are likely the [[event:3543|Philistines]] of the Hebrew Bible. Other groups may have founded or merged with coastal populations across the Mediterranean. Some scholars interpret the Sea Peoples not primarily as external invaders but as refugees and migrants displaced by droughts, internal rebellions, and system collapses within the Bronze Age world — simultaneously victims and perpetrators of the broader collapse.

The Sea Peoples episode remains one of history's most dramatic examples of how interconnected civilizational systems can cascade into catastrophic collapse when multiple stresses converge simultaneously. The destruction they caused or participated in ended the international Bronze Age order that had integrated the Near East, Egypt, and the Aegean for centuries, giving way to a new world of smaller, less complex polities in what scholars call the Early Iron Age.
